answer:
**The opening couplet (sher/matla) of Mirza Ghalib's ghazal "Koi ummeed bar nahin aati" (Diwan-E-Ghalib, ch. 115) is the most famous for its line on hopelessness.**[^1]

This couplet translates as:

> No hope I have is ever answered,[^2]  
> No way ahead can be uncovered.[^3]

It directly embodies the theme of dashed hopes ("koi ummeed bar nahin aati"), serving as the ghazal's title, refrain signature, and opening lines.[^4] The wiki analysis flags it prominently (e.g., as the opening and a LexRank key passage), with distinctive trigrams like "hope ever answered" and original Urdu phrases like "koi ummeed bar." While the ghazal repeats "nahin aati" (does not come) across its 10 couplets to evoke elusive desires (sleep, laughter, piety, death, shame), this first couplet is iconic for hopelessness.[^5]

[^1]: Mirza Ghalib, *Diwan-E-Ghalib*, ch. 115 ("Koi ummeed bar nahin aati"), [chapter translation](https://falsafa.corpus/works/mirza-ghalib-diwan-e-ghalib-74ed4c/115-koi-ummeed-bar-nahin-aati/translation/).
[^2]: Ibid., [p-92c600](https://falsafa.corpus/works/mirza-ghalib-diwan-e-ghalib-74ed4c/115-koi-ummeed-bar-nahin-aati/translation/#p-92c600).
[^3]: Ibid.
[^4]: Ibid., wiki card/full sheet.
[^5]: Ibid., transliteration: "Koi ummeed bar nahin aati / Koi soorat nazar nahin aati" ([p-5116c9](https://falsafa.corpus/works/mirza-ghalib-diwan-e-ghalib-74ed4c/115-koi-ummeed-bar-nahin-aati/transliteration/#p-5116c9)).
